title: Chorus, phaser, distortion, and bitcrusher: creative effects explained
dek: Beyond EQ and compression, Tracks ships four creative effects that add movement, grit, and texture to a sound: chorus, phaser, distortion, and bitcrusher. Here is what each one does, how it works, and when to reach for it.

title: How to use a noise gate to clean up noisy recordings
dek: A noise gate mutes a track whenever its signal drops below a threshold, cutting hiss, room noise, and bleed between the notes you actually played. Here is how Tracks' built-in gate works and how to set it up on a vocal or instrument track.

title: De-esser explained: how to fix harsh S and T sounds in vocals
dek: A de-esser is a frequency-specific compressor that tames the harsh sibilant sounds in a vocal take without dulling the rest of the voice. Here is how sibilance happens and how to dial in Tracks' built-in de-esser so vocals sit smoothly in a mix.

title: How to comp the best take from multiple recordings
dek: Take comping means recording several passes of a vocal or instrument part and stitching the best sections of each into one final take. Here is how count-in recording and take comping work in Tracks so you never settle for a single imperfect performance.

title: How to write a bassline that locks with the kick drum
dek: A bassline that locks with the kick shares rhythmic space instead of colliding with it. Here is a practical method for placing bass notes around your kick drum in the Tracks piano roll so the low end stays tight instead of muddy.

title: What is the session view? Loop-based songwriting in a browser DAW
dek: The session view lets you launch and combine loop clips live instead of arranging on a fixed timeline. Here is what Tracks' clip-launch view does, when to use it instead of the timeline, and how to turn a jam into a finished arrangement.

title: Subtractive vs FM synthesis: how to design your own sounds
dek: Subtractive synthesis carves a sound out of a raw waveform with a filter, and FM synthesis builds a tone by using one oscillator to modulate another. Here is how each works in the Tracks browser synth and how to program your own patch from scratch.

title: How to write chord progressions in the piano roll
dek: Chords give a song its emotional color and its harmonic backbone. Here is a practical, no-theory-degree way to build chord progressions in the Tracks piano roll, from picking a key to voicing and looping them.

title: Time-stretch and repitch explained: change tempo or key without ruining quality
dek: Time-stretch changes a clip's length without changing its pitch, and repitch changes its pitch without changing its length. Here is how both work in the Tracks browser studio and when to use each one.

title: How to chop and flip a sample into a beat
dek: Sample chopping cuts a longer audio clip into short one-shots so you can play them in a new order, on a new grid, in a new key. Here is how to chop and flip a sample using the Tracks 16-pad sampler with no downloads.

title: Quantize and swing explained: how to make a beat groove
dek: Quantize snaps notes to the grid for tightness, and swing shifts alternating notes late for feel. Here is how to use both together in the Tracks piano roll and drum machine to make a beat sound programmed instead of robotic.

title: How to export a MIDI file from your browser DAW
dek: Exporting a standard MIDI file gets the notes out of Tracks and into any other instrument, notation app, or DAW. Here is how MIDI export works, what it captures, and what it leaves behind.

title: How to use group buses and sends to organize a mix
dek: Group buses collect related tracks under one fader and one effect chain, and sends let multiple tracks share a single reverb or delay. Here is how to set both up in Tracks to keep a busy mix organized and controllable.

title: How to arrange a song: verse, chorus, bridge structure
dek: A song needs a structure before it needs polish. Here is how to block out verses, choruses, bridges, and transitions on the Tracks timeline so a pile of loops turns into a finished track.

title: Where does your project actually live? Local storage in a browser DAW
dek: Tracks never uploads your project to a server. Here is how local, in-browser storage works, why it means no account and no cloud, and how to back up or move a project safely.

title: How to master a track for streaming: loudness basics
dek: Mastering a track for streaming means gain-staging your mix, gluing it together on a bus, and watching a loudness meter instead of guessing. Here is how to do it in the free Tracks browser studio.

title: How to use automation to make your mix move
dek: Static volume and effect settings make a mix feel flat. Here is how to draw automation curves for volume, pan, and effect sends in Tracks so your track builds, breathes, and changes over time.

title: Best free VST alternative instruments in the browser
dek: You don't need paid VST plugins to get good sounds — Tracks ships a subtractive synth, an FM synth, 808/909 drum machines, a 16-pad sampler, and SoundFont playback built into the browser, free.

title: BandLab vs Soundtrap vs Tracks: free browser studios compared
dek: BandLab, Soundtrap, and Tracks are the three free browser DAWs most people encounter — here is a plain comparison of instruments, collaboration, export formats, and data privacy so you can pick the right studio for your workflow.

title: How to make a beat for a rap song
dek: Making a rap beat starts with a kick-snare pattern, a sample or synth hook, and the right tempo — here is how to program all three in the Tracks browser studio with no downloads or software install.

title: How to split a song into stems (vocals, drums, bass)
dek: Stem splitting separates a finished audio file into isolated parts — vocals, drums, bass, and other — so you can remix, sample, or study any element. Tracks runs this extraction entirely in your browser with no upload and no account.

title: Sidechain compression explained (the "pumping" effect)
dek: Sidechain compression makes a track duck in volume whenever another track hits — it's the pumping effect you hear in dance music, and you can dial it in with Tracks' built-in sidechain ducking in minutes.

title: How to make a melody if you don't know music theory
dek: You don't need to read music to write a melody that works — here is a practical method for building a memorable tune in the Tracks piano roll using shapes, repetition, and your ear alone.

title: Free online music maker for students and Chromebooks
dek: Chromebooks block software installs, but tracks.jfound.net runs in any browser tab — here is how students can record, arrange, and export a full song with no account and no download.

title: How to use a MIDI keyboard in a browser DAW (Web MIDI)
dek: Connect your MIDI keyboard to your browser and play, record, and sequence with it in Tracks — no drivers, no software install, just the Web MIDI API and a USB cable.

title: Make lo-fi hip-hop in your browser
dek: Lo-fi hip-hop is built from slow, swinging drums, a warm bass line, a dusty chord loop, and a vinyl-style texture — and you can make all of it inside Tracks with no downloads.

title: How to export your song to MP3 or WAV
dek: Exporting your finished track from Tracks takes two clicks — here is how to choose between MP3 and WAV, what the settings mean, and how to get the best quality file for streaming, sharing, or sending to a mastering engineer.

title: What is a DAW, and do you need an expensive one?
dek: A DAW (digital audio workstation) is software for recording, arranging, and mixing music. Here is what the core tools do, what the difference between free and paid options really is, and why a browser DAW can do the job for most producers.

title: How to mix a track: EQ, compression, and reverb basics
dek: Learn the fundamentals of mixing a track in your browser — EQ to shape tone, compression to control dynamics, and reverb to add space — with no plugins and no downloads.

title: Free online drum machine: program beats with no software
dek: Step by step guide to programming drum beats in your browser using Tracks' free online drum machine — 808, 909, swing, velocity, and export with no download or account.

title: How to make a song in your browser for beginners
dek: A step-by-step beginner's guide to making a complete song in your browser — arrangement, melody, drums, and export — with no software and no account.

title: The best free online music studio (no download, no account)
dek: What a browser-based music studio can actually do in 2026 — and how to make a full track in one without spending a cent.

title: How to make beats in your browser (step by step)
dek: Build a beat from scratch using the drum machine, sampler, and piano roll — no plugins, no install.

title: A free GarageBand alternative that runs in any browser
dek: No Mac? No problem. Here's how Tracks compares to GarageBand and other desktop DAWs — and where a browser studio wins.

title: How to record and tune vocals in your browser
dek: Record a vocal take through your mic, comp the best parts, and snap it to key with built-in autotune — all online and free.
